origin.question: Two stars of mass $\mathbf{M}_1$ and $\mathbf{M}_2$ separated by a distance $\mathbf{R}$ revolve about each other in a nonrelativistic circular orbit. Due to gravitational radiation reaction, $\mathbf{R}$ changes with time. Find $\mathbf{R}(t)$ .
origin.solution: Solution 18.6. Let $\mathfrak{m}_1, \mathfrak{m}_2$ and $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ be respectively the masses, and distances from the center of mass, of the two stars rotating at angular frequency $\omega$ . From Newtonian mechanics we know that $\mathbf{r}_1\mathfrak{m}_1 = \mathbf{r}_2\mathfrak{m}_2 = \mathbb{R}\mu$ , where $\mathbb{R} \equiv \mathbf{r}_1 + \mathbf{r}_2$ and $\mu = \text{reduced mass} = \mathfrak{m}_1\mathfrak{m}_2 / (\mathfrak{m}_1 + \mathfrak{m}_2)$ . The total energy of the stars can now be written as a function of $\mathbb{M}$ , $\mathbb{R}$ , and $\mu$ :$$\mathbf {E} = \frac {1} {2} \mathbf {r_{1}}^{2} \mathbf {m_{1}} + \frac {1} {2} \mathbf {r_{2}}^{2} \mathbf {m_{2}} \quad \omega^{2} - \frac {\mathbf {m_{1}} \mathbf {m_{2}}} {\mathbf {R}} = \frac {1} {2} \omega^{2} \mathbf {R} \mu - \frac {\mu \mathbf {M}} {\mathbf {R}} = - \frac {1} {2} \frac {\mu \mathbf {M}} {\mathbf {R}} .$$Here $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{m}_1 + \mathbf{m}_2$ and Kepler's laws have been used.The power dissipated as gravitational waves is easily calculated once the reduced quadrupole moment,$$\mathbf {\Phi}_{j k} \equiv \int \rho \left(\mathbf {x}_{j} \mathbf {x}_{k} - \frac {1} {3} \mathbf {\nabla r}^{2} \delta_{j k}\right) \mathrm {d}^{3} \mathbf {x},$$is evaluated. Note that the "reducing term" $\int \frac{1}{3} \rho r^2 \delta_{jk} d^3 x$ will be constant in time and can be ignored. Suppose the $z$ axis to be the axis of rotation and $\phi$ the azimuthal angle from the $x$ -axis to the line joining the stars, then (aside from time independent terms) we have$$\begin{array}{l} I_{x x} = \left(r_{1}^{2} m_{1} + r_{2}^{2} m_{2}\right) \cos^{2} \phi = \frac {1} {2} R^{2} \mu \cos 2 \phi + c o n s t a n t \\ I_{y y} = - \frac {1} {2} R^{2} \mu \cos 2 \phi + c o n s t a n t \\ I_{x y} = I_{y x} = \frac {1} {2} R^{2} \mu \sin 2 \phi . \\ \end{array}$$Since $\phi = \omega t$ , it is straightforward to differentiate three times, perform the sum $\mathbf{\Phi}_{jk}\mathbf{\Phi}_{jk}$ and time average to find$$\begin{array}{l} \frac {1} {5} < \ddot {\tau}_{j k} \ddot {\tau}_{j k} > = \frac {1} {5} (2 \omega )^{6} \left(\frac {1} {2} R^{2} \mu\right)^{2} < \sin^{2} 2 \omega t + \sin^{2} 2 \omega t + 2 \cos^{2} 2 \omega t > \\ = \frac {3 2} {5} \omega^{6} R^{4} \mu^{2} = \frac {3 2} {5} \frac {M^{3} \mu^{2}} {R^{5}} = P_{G W} . \\ \end{array}$$Since this power is radiated at the expense of orbital energy, it follows that$$\frac {\mathrm {d E}} {\mathrm {d t}} = \frac {1} {2} \frac {\mu \mathrm {M}} {\mathrm {R}^{2}} \frac {\mathrm {d R}} {\mathrm {d t}} = - \mathrm {P_{G W}} = - \frac {3 2} {5} \frac {\mathrm {M}^{3} \mu^{2}} {\mathrm {R}^{5}} .$$The resulting differential equation$$\mathrm {R}^{3} \frac {\mathrm {d R}} {\mathrm {d t}} = - \frac {6 4} {5} \mathrm {M}^{2} \mu$$is easily integrated to give$$R^{4} = - (2 5 6 / 5) M^{2} \mu t + c o n s t a n t.$$ If we denote by $t_0$ the time at which the separation goes to zero according to this formula, then$$\mathsf {R}^{4} = \frac {2 5 6} {5} \mathsf {M}^{2} \mu (\mathsf {t}_{0} - \mathsf {t} ) .$$ We can find the time $t_0$ by noting that at $t = 0$ , $R$ must be the present separation $R_{\text{now}}$ , thus$$t_{0} = \frac {5 R_{\mathrm {n o w}}^{4}} {2 5 6 \mathrm {M}^{2} \mu} .$$
